summary: Powerful late‑1971 set with monster “The Other One” > “Wharf Rat”
blurb: The band delivers a tight first set and launches into an epic second‑set suite of The Other One into Wharf Rat. Jerry’s vocals on Wharf Rat and Sugaree are among his best, while Keith’s piano weaves beautifully with Pigpen. The Betty soundboard capture is crystal clear, making this a must‑listen for Deadheads.
review: December 14, 1971 at Hill Auditorium captures the Grateful Dead in a pivotal moment, straddling the raw energy of ’71 and the emerging spaciousness of ’72. The first set rolls through classics—Truckin’, Sugaree, Mr. Charlie, Tennessee Jed, Jack Straw—with tight rhythm work from Bob and a vocal peak for Jerry on Wharf Rat and Sugaree that reviewers call “some of his best ever.”  The second set is the show’s centerpiece: a soaring The Other One suite that morphs seamlessly into Wharf Rat, followed by a jubilant Sugar Magnolia and two high‑energy Not Fade Away repeats. Keith Godchaux’s piano is expressive and perfectly locked in with Pigpen, while Phil’s angular bass runs rule the middle jams. Bill Kreutzmann’s drums are crisp and present, giving the improvisations a solid pocket that makes you want to dance. The Betty soundboard source (gd71‑12‑14.sbd.deibert.12763.sbeok.shnf) delivers crystal‑clear instrument separation and an “awesome” mix, highlighting every nuance of this legendary performance.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings use unequivocally positive language ("monster", "legendary", "scorching guitar", "crisp drums") and only one is mixed, with no consistent negative criticism of the performance. The consensus praises The Other One suite, vocal highlights, and overall tightness, justifying a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Betty soundboard capture is crystal‑clear with superb instrument separation, crisp drums and flawless mix that showcases the band’s peak performance.

key_highlights: Monumental The Other One → Wharf Rat suite signals the band's shift toward their 1972 sound.
Crystal‑clear Betty board provides excellent instrument separation, especially the crisp drum presence.
Jerry's vocals on Wharf Rat and Sugaree are highlighted as some of his best ever.
song_highlights: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad

band_performance.Jerry: Scorching guitar tone on The Other One and Wharf Rat; vocals on Wharf Rat and Sugaree called among his best ever.
band_performance.Phil: Angular bass runs that rule the middle jams; powerful intro on The Other One and solid groove throughout.
band_performance.Bob: Tight rhythm work, especially during interwoven leads; confident lead vocals on Jack Straw and strong singing across the set.
band_performance.Keith: Expressive piano with beautiful fills; magical chemistry with Pigpen adds depth to the improvisations.
band_performance.Billy: Crisp, stellar drum presence; solid pocket groove that drives the jams.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
